# The Five Signal Layers

| Signal layer | What it controls | Examples of proxy signals | What manipulation does |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility | What gets seen | views, shares, impressions, rankings, chart position, search placement | Makes an object appear more widely demanded or relevant than it is |
| Credibility | What gets trusted | reviews, testimonials, follower counts, endorsements, trust badges, customer logos | Makes an object appear validated by independent or expert sources |
| Conversion | What appears to be demand | clicks, leads, installs, trials, signups, purchases, waitlist size | Makes dashboards show demand that may be fake, low-quality, coerced, or misattributed |
| Attribution | Who gets credit | last click, affiliate cookie, install attribution, postback source, campaign source | Moves money to actors who did not create real value |
| Legitimacy | What appears institutionally validated | ARR, funding, accelerator status, launch badges, compliance claims, press mentions | Converts distorted proof into prestige, capital, trust, and recruiting advantage |

## Layer interactions

Manufactured traction often works because these layers reinforce each other.

Example: fake reviews improve credibility, which improves conversion, which improves marketplace rank, which improves visibility, which creates more real purchases. A synthetic signal can bootstrap into partial reality.

Example: clipping creates visibility, which creates perceived cultural momentum, which leads to real audience attention, which then creates more legitimate engagement. This creates a laundering cycle where manufactured attention becomes harder to separate from real attention.

## Key point

The unethical part is not always that every later signal is fake. Often the unethical part is that the first signal was engineered deceptively, and later real behaviour was induced by a false representation of organic demand.
